message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (different types, no block)" — expects { premium: 80 } (2×25 + 1×25 + 5 fee)
Expected behavior: moonstone is a component priced 25G each like rune; different types do not form a block together. Add moonstone:25 to base premium table.
Current error: expected { premium: 155 } to deeply equal { premium: 80 }
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Let me read the spec for context on moonstone pricing.

content.content:
20:| component (rune, moonstone) | 250 G each | 25 G each |
25:(rune ≠ moonstone). Block applies only at exactly 3; 4+ alike are priced
56:- Components (rune/moonstone) have no enchantment/material → no special clause.
83:- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G (no block, different types)
84:- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (two separate blocks: 60 + 60)
169:Item types: `sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone`. Item
181:- ❓ "Alike" components → **exactly the same type** (rune ≠ moonstone).
204:9. 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 — R2 different types, no block.
205:10. 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 — R2 two separate blocks.

content.text:
The spec confirms moonstone is a component priced 25G each, same as rune. The current error is `155` because moonstone falls to the default 100. The minimal fix is to add `moonstone: 25` to the base premium table.

The current `groupByType` logic already handles different types correctly (runes and moonstone are separate groups), so just adding the table entry is the minimal change.

content.text:
All 11 tests pass. Let me verify no over-implementation: the next todo is "3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (two separate blocks of 60)". With my change, 3 moonstones would now form a block of 60 (since `basePremiumForGroup` already has the block-of-3 rule). Let me check whether that next test would already pass.

The next test groups 3 runes (block → 60) + 3 moonstones (block → 60) = 120 + 5 fee = 125. The current code WOULD already handle this. However, this is unavoidable: the block logic was generalized in cycle 9 (driven by the rune block test), and adding `moonstone: 25` is the minimal change to make the current test pass — I cannot make moonstone work as a 25G component without it being in the table, and once it is a normal type, the existing group/block logic naturally applies. Adding moonstone is the smallest possible change; the block behavior is pre-existing generalized code, not new anticipation.

Now I'll append to the journal.

content.text:
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: Added `moonstone: 25` to the `BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` table in `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_12-26-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`.
**Result**: All tests now pass (11 passing, 32 todo).
**Approach**: This is the minimal change. Previously moonstone fell to the default 100G base, producing 155 instead of 80. The spec prices moonstone as a 25G component identical to rune, and the existing `groupByType` logic already treats different types as separate groups (no block forms between rune and moonstone). So a single table entry was all that was demanded — no new branching or algorithm.

Note on anticipation: the next todo ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → two blocks of 60") will pass immediately once activated, because moonstone now reuses the block-of-exactly-3 logic generalized back in cycle 9. This is unavoidable rather than premature — adding moonstone to the table is the smallest possible change to satisfy the current test; the block behavior is pre-existing code, not newly added anticipation.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
